coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots modified bismuth antimonate for broad spectrum photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants: Boosted charge separation, DFT calculations and mechanism unveiling
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhuangzhuang
Cheng, Qiang
Wang, Xiaotian
Li, Jiaming
Li, Wenxuan
Li, Yuan
Zhang, Gaoke
coredata.dc:description: Developing a photocatalyst that simultaneously has broad spectral response and strong redox property of photoinduced carriers still remains challenging. Herein, carbon dots modified BiSbO4 composites (CDs-BiSbO4) were synthesized via a simple ultrasonication-calcination method without changing the electron property in the bulk of BiSbO4 and perfectly reserving its strong oxidation ability. The admirable up-converted property of CDs efficiently converts long wavelength from 550 to 900 nm to short wavelength from 320 to 500 nm, endowing BiSbO4 with a broad spectral response property. Moreover, CDs can serve as electron sinks, greatly inhibiting the recombination of photoinduced carriers originated from BiSbO4. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations together validate a strong electron screening effect exists between the interlayer of CDs and BiSbO4, forming an ultrahigh electron transfer tunnel at the interlayer. More interestingly, the adsorption energy (Eads) of O2 onto CDs (−0.338 eV) was more negative than that onto BiSbO4 (−0.230 eV), demonstrating the O2 adsorbed on the CDs is more stable than that on BiSbO4. When O2 molecules are adsorbed on the surface of CDs, all electrons at the interlayer are inclined to accumulate on O2, offering an ideal platform for the activation of molecular oxygen. This work offers an idea for constructing novel photocatalysts with both powerful redox property and broad spectral response for efficient photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Broad spectral response
CDs-BiSbO4 composites
Up-converted property
DFT calculations
Photocatalytic degradation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC No. 51472194 ) and the NSF of Hubei Province ( 2016CFA078 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs under bright field. (b) Particle size distribution of CDs. (c) The geometric structure of CDs. (d) TEM image of 2 CDBSb. (e) HRTEM image of 2 CDBSb. (f) TEM image of 2 CDBSb and EDS elemental mappings of (g) Bi, (h) O, (i) Sb and (j) C.

(a) XRD patterns of different catalysts. (b) XRD pattern of CDs, inset: High-definition spectrum 2θ = 20°–40° of pristine BiSbO4 and 8 CDBSb. (c) Raman spectrum of CDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of different catalysts.
Photoluminescent spectra of CDs water solution.

Photocatalytic degradation curves of RhB over different catalysts (a) under simulated sunlight irradiation and (b) under a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter. Photocatalytic degradation curves of CIP over different catalysts (c) under simulated sunlight irradiation and (d) under a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter.
Possible degradation pathways of RhB over the optimal sample.
Possible degradation pathways of CIP over the optimal sample.

(a) UV–vis DRS spectra of different catalysts, inset: white color sample represents pristine BiSbO4 and black-gray color represents 8 CDBSb. (b) Bandgap energy of pristine BiSbO4. (c) XPS valence band spectrum of pristine BiSbO4, inset: Mott-Schottky plot of BiSbO4. (d) Potential locations vs NHE (V) of pristine BiSbO4.

(a) PL spectra of pristine BiSbO4 and 2 CDBSb. (b) Photocurrent signals of BiSbO4 and 2 CDBSb under simulated sunlight irradiation. (c) Photocurrent signals of BiSbO4 and 2 CDBSb under the irradiation of a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter. (d) EIS spectra of pristine BiSbO4 and 2 CDBSb.
High resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s and (d) Sb 3d.

(a) TDOS and (b) PDOS of BiSbO4. (c) Charge difference density of CDs/BiSbO4 layer and (d) corresponding planar averaged charge difference density along z axis. (e) Charge difference density of O2/CDs/BiSbO4 layer and (f) corresponding planar averaged charge difference density along z axis. Charge accumulation is in yellow and depletion in blue.

(a) Charge difference distribution of optimized O2 adsorption on BiSbO4 (b) work function of BiSbO4-O2 layer. (c) Charge difference distribution of optimized O2 adsorption on CDs and (d) work function of CDs-O2 layer. Charge accumulation is in yellow and depletion in blue, Eads represents the adsorption energy.

ESR signals of (a) DMPO-O2 −, (b) DMPO-OH and (c) semi-quantitative O2 − concentration under simulated sunlight irradiation. ESR signals of (d) DMPO-O2 −, (e) DMPO-OH and (f) semi-quantitative O2 − concentration under the irradiation of a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots modified bismuth antimonate for broad spectrum photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants: Boosted charge separation, DFT calculations and mechanism unveiling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Mineral Resources Processing and Environment, State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, 122 Luoshi Road, Wuhan 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Mineral Resources Processing and Environment
State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Institute of Advanced Technology, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450052, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Institute of Advanced Technology, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450052, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Hubei Key Laboratory of Mineral Resources Processing and Environment, State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, 122 Luoshi Road, Wuhan 430070, China (G. Zhang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Mineral Resources Processing and Environment
State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a photocatalyst that simultaneously has broad spectral response and strong redox property of photoinduced carriers still remains challenging. Herein, carbon dots modified BiSbO4 composites (CDs-BiSbO4) were synthesized via a simple ultrasonication-calcination method without changing the electron property in the bulk of BiSbO4 and perfectly reserving its strong oxidation ability. The admirable up-converted property of CDs efficiently converts long wavelength from 550 to 900 nm to short wavelength from 320 to 500 nm, endowing BiSbO4 with a broad spectral response property. Moreover, CDs can serve as electron sinks, greatly inhibiting the recombination of photoinduced carriers originated from BiSbO4. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations together validate a strong electron screening effect exists between the interlayer of CDs and BiSbO4, forming an ultrahigh electron transfer tunnel at the interlayer. More interestingly, the adsorption energy (Eads) of O2 onto CDs (−0.338 eV) was more negative than that onto BiSbO4 (−0.230 eV), demonstrating the O2 adsorbed on the CDs is more stable than that on BiSbO4. When O2 molecules are adsorbed on the surface of CDs, all electrons at the interlayer are inclined to accumulate on O2, offering an ideal platform for the activation of molecular oxygen. This work offers an idea for constructing novel photocatalysts with both powerful redox property and broad spectral response for efficient photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: A photocatalyst with both robust redox ability of photoexcited carriers and broad spectral response is essentially an irreconcilable contradiction. Recent years, large amounts of researches are merely focused on broadening the spectral response range of a photocatalyst by scarfing the redox ability of photoexcited carriers as a prerequisite. Actually, this is not a very wise choice from the perspective of carriers’ dynamics [1]. Especially for wide band-gap photocatalysts, although this kind of materials possess strong reduction or oxidation ability, few works have been reported to develop the photocatalytic performance of them because of their pale light harvesting performance. Thus, designing an easily tunable method for boosting the light absorption without changing the carriers’ strong redox ability of a wide bandgap photocatalyst is of great significance.

Bismuth Antimonate (BiSbO4), as a typical wide band-gap photocatalyst, has attracted many researchers for its strong oxidation ability (VB about 3.2 eV) application in the treatment of organic pollutants including some volatile organic compounds and persistent pollution sources. The superior photocatalytic performance of BiSbO4 is due to its unique crystal structure. The monoclinic BiSbO4 has a layered structure, sharing an SbO6 octahedron at the top corners. The Bi atoms are located in adjacent layers and form a BiO8 polyhedron shared with the surrounding eight O atoms. SbO6 and BiO8 polyhedrons have different degrees of lattice distortions. The distorted SbO6 and BiO8 polyhedrons can induce the existence of dipole moments, forming two different internal electric fields which promotes the separation of photogenerated carriers. Moreover, the coexistence of two internal electric fields may have a synergistic effect, thereby further promoting the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Pristine BiSbO4 has a wide bandgap value about 3.55 eV, signifying photoexcited electrons or holes have a strong reduction or oxidation ability. Unfortunately, the biggest shortboard BiSbO4 encounters is also before-mentioned weak light absorption, indicating pristine BiSbO4 can merely exert its photocatalytic performance under ultraviolet region [2,3]. Usually, several well-known methods, including constructing heterojunction, doping and triggering defect have been utilized to modify a pristine photocatalyst to make it possess wide spectral response property [4–11]. However, constructing heterojunction essentially can’t change the spectral response range of a single photocatalyst, which can merely promote the separation of photoinduced carries [12–16]. Moreover, traditional type Ⅱ heterostructured photocatalysts can’t retain the strong redox ability of photoinduced carriers. In a sense, although doping with ions or triggering defect can broaden the light absorption of a photocatalyst, the regulation of doping concentration and defect concentration is uneasy [17–19]. Therefore, finding a reasonable method for modifying a pristine photocatalyst and making it with both strong redox ability of photoinduced carriers and strong light absorption is of great urgency.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a kind of nanosized C-based material with diameter less than 10 nm [20–25]. CDs are mainly constituted of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms with some surface functional groups. Recent years, CDs have attracted intense interest because of their many unique and novel properties, particularly, their high aqueous solubility, environmental compatibility, low toxicity and unique electron reservoir [26–30]. Therefore, CDs can serve as an ideal candidate to couple with a pristine photocatalyst for enhancing its photocatalytic performance. Besides, owing to the outstanding up-converted photoluminescence (PL) behavior of CDs, the light absorption range of a pristine photocatalyst can be also broadened after coupling them together. More importantly, in comparison with other modifying methods of a photocatalyst, such as, doping and triggering vacancy, the introduction of CDs on the surface of a photocatalyst is controllable and the preparation process is simple. To date, many works about CDs-based photocatalysts have been reported, such as CDs/TiO2, CDs/BiWO4, CDs/BiVO4, and CDs/BiOBr, etc. [31–39]. However, among these reports, CDs are seldom combined with a wide bandgap semiconductor, such as BiSbO4, to amplify its light absorption range and greatly boost its charge separation.

Considering the merits of CDs and the shortboards of pristine BiSbO4, we successfully constructed CDs/BiSbO4 composites for the first time. The composites exhibit remarkably enhanced photocatalytic performance than pristine BiSbO4 either under simulated sunlight illumination or under the irradiation of a Xe lamp with 550 nm filter. More importantly, the coupling of CDs and BiSbO4 didn’t change the VBM (valence band maximum) and CBM (conduction band minimum) locations of BiSbO4 and retain its strong oxidation property. Various characterization techniques were also utilized to study the morphologies, constitutions, optical and photoelectrochemical properties of the composites. Besides, an ideal CDs-BiSbO4 slab was constructed and the electrons transfer tendency was discussed from atomic scale based on the DFT calculations.

CDs were successfully fabricated via a simple hydrothermal method. Specifically, 1.5 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of urea were dissolved into 30 ml of deionized water and sealed at a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was transferred into an oven and maintained at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the liquid was collected and centrifugated to remove large particles. After that, the liquid was dialyzed for 100 h, followed by freeze-drying to obtain the brown-black powders. Pristine BiSbO4 were synthesized referring to our previous work [40].

Briefly, 0.0025, 0.01, 0.02 and 0.04 g of CDs powders were transferred into 4 different beakers, respectively. Then, each beaker was added into 0.5 g of BiSbO4 powders and 20 ml of ethanol solution. After that, the 4 mixed liquid suspensions were ultrasonicated for 4 h, subsequently transferred to four alumina crucibles, and dried in a fuming cupboard. The crucibles were then transferred into a muffle furnace and calcinated at 300 °C for 4 h. The collected gray-black powders were washed with water and ethanol for several times and dried at an oven overnight. The as-prepared products were denoted as 0.5, 2, 4 and 8 CDBSb, respectively.

The crystallinities of the catalysts were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements on a D/Max-B X-ray diffractometer (voltage 40 kV, electrical current 40 mA) using Cu Kα radiation, with a scanned area of 2θ ranging from 10 to 70°. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a Nexus (Thermo Nicolet, U.S.A.) from 400 to 4000 cm−1. Raman spectra were recorded using an INVIA (RENISHAW, U.K.). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with a Hitachi S-8010. The crystal planes and fringes of the samples were obtained by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM 2100F). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a PHI Quantera II system spectrometer to investigate the valence states of the catalysts. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) was tested with a UV–vis spectrometer (UV-4100). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with a RF-5301 PC, Shimadzu (Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 312 nm. Photoelectrochemical tests were measured on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660C) with a typical three-electrode system. The electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements were obtained on a Bruker ER200-SRC spectrometer.

Photocatalytic activities of catalysts were evaluated via the degradation of RhB (Rhodamine B) and CIP (Ciprofloxacin). The procedure for the degradation of RhB was performed as follows: 0.1 g of as-prepared sample was firstly added into 100 ml of RhB aqueous solution (10 mg/L) and then the suspension was stirred in the darkness for 30 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After that, the suspension was illuminated by simulated sunlight and a Xe lamp with a 550 nm filter (PLS-SXE300D, Beijing Perfect light Technology Co., Ltd), respectively. At a fixed interval (20 min for simulated sunlight and 30 min for a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter), 5 ml of the suspension was taken out and centrifugated to obtain the supernatant. Then, the absorbance of the supernatant was analyzed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at its maximum absorption wavelength of 554 nm. For CIP aqueous solution (10 mg/L), the concentration of its supernatant was analyzed using a full-spectrum UV–vis spectrophotometer and other conditions are same.

ESR spectroscopy was performed to detect the signals of active substances. The process was as follows: 40 mg of catalysts were added into two sample tubes, respectively. Then, 1 ml of deionized water was injected into one of the sample tubes for the detection of OH. Another sample tube was injected with 1 ml of methanol for the detection of O2 −. After that, 30 μL of 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) was injected into above sample tubes. Subsequently, after 5 min irradiation by simulated sunlight or a Xe lamp with a 550 nm cut-off filter, the solution immediately detected by the ESR detection machine. The O2 − concentration was further quantified by the dissipation of 5 mg/L NBT (nitro blue tetrazolium). NBT can react with O2 − (1 mmol O2 − depletes 4 mmol NBT) and chromogenic reaction of blue can appear. The absorbance of the blue color solution was detected with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 259 nm.

VASP (Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package) was utilized to perform the electron properties of BiSbO4 and electron transfer property at the interlayer of CDs-BiSbO4 layer. Before static calculations, the BiSbO4 primitive cell and CDs-BiSbO4 layer were firstly optimized to attain their most stable structures with lowest energy. The parameters in the process of calculation were set as follows: The exchange-correlation between electrons is treated through using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) in the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) form. The cut-off energy was set to 520 eV. The convergence criteria of electronic relaxation and ion relaxation were set to 0.001 eV/cell and 1 meV/atom, respectively. The k-point mesh was set to 5 × 1 × 1, which is centered at Gamma point. The adsorption energy (Eads) was calculated according to the following equation below.
E ads
=
E tol
-
E slab
+
E mol
Etol, Eslab, and Emol referred to the convergent energy of the adsorption structures, isolated BiSbO4 or CDs structure, and O2 molecule.

Fig. S1a–d are SEM images of 0.5 CDBSb, 2 CDBSb, 4 CDBSb and 8 CDBSb, respectively. In comparison with pristine BiSbO4, the morphology of CDBSb composites have no any change and still displays a uniform bundle-like shape. Fig. 1 a is TEM image of CDs. Obviously, CDs display a round shape with diameter ranging from 2 to 12 nm and are distributed very evenly. Moreover, the diameter of most CDs is less than 10 nm, which can be validated by the particle size distribution in the Fig. 1b. Fig. 1c is a typical geometric structure of CDs. Fig. 1d and e are TEM and HRTEM images of 2 CDBSb, respectively. Clearly, CDs with diameter about 3 nm are uniformly attached on the surface of BiSbO4. The lattice spacings of 0.32 nm and 0.244 nm are ascribed to (002) crystal plane of CDs and (020) crystal plane of BiSbO4, respectively. Fig. 1f–j are EDS (element distribution mappings) of 2 CDBSb. Apparently, Bi, O, Sb and C elements do exist in the CDBSb and the proportion of C element is lowest. The energy spectrum of these elements is displayed in Fig. S2b. The SEM and TEM analyses validate the CDs are successfully introduced on the surface of BiSbO4.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks at 2θ degrees of 27.34°, 30.78°, 33.34°, 36.75°, 48.7°, 50.59°, and 54.02° are ascribed to characteristic peaks of monoclinic BiSbO4 (PDF card: JCPD 48-0469). Obviously, compared with pristine BiSbO4, the XRD patterns of CDBSb composites have no obvious change, demonstrating the introduction of CDs has no influence on the crystal structure of BiSbO4. The diffraction peaks located at 2θ value of 26° for CDs (Fig. 2b) are too weak to be observed, which is due to low contents of CDs in the composites. Moreover, the inset in Fig. 2b indicates that the diffraction degree of CDBSb slightly shifts to lower value, which is ascribed to the extrusion of CDs. Fig. 2c is the Raman spectrum of CDs. Obviously, two strong peaks located at 1603 cm−1 and 1361 cm−1 belong to G band and D band of carbon, respectively, which is in accordance with sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbons [15]. Fig. 2d shows the FT-IR spectra of BiSbO4 and CDBSb composites. The absorption band located at 775 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of Sb–O [2]. The absorption band positioned at 3434 cm−1 is ascribed to typical stretching vibration of H2O adsorbed on the surface of samples. After the introduction of CDs, some new functional groups appear. The new emerging absorption bands located at 1570 cm−1 and 1723 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of CO and COOH, respectively [23]. From above characterization analyses, it is concluded that CDs/BiSbO4 composites are successfully synthesized.

Considering the possible up-converted property of as-prepared CDs, the photoluminescence spectra of its water solution were recorded and displayed in Fig. 3 . The excitation wavelength is from 300 nm to 900 nm and the interval between every two excitation wavelengths is 50 nm. Obviously, when the excitation wavelength is above 550 nm, the emission wavelength of CDs solution exhibits an apparent blue shift and the minimum emission wavelength is about 320 nm. After several repeated measurements, the emission wavelength of CDs water solution is about 320 nm–500 nm, signifying the as-prepared CDs possess a great up-converted property. When the CDs water solution was illuminated by a 365 nm UV lamp, it also emits a bright blue light, which is a characteristic of CDs solution, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3. The introduction of CDs on the surface of BiSbO4 can broaden the spectral response of pristine BiSbO4, which can be further demonstrated by the discussion of its photocatalytic performance.

Firstly, RhB was chosen to appraise the photocatalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts. Fig. 4 a presents the photocatalytic degradation curves over different samples under simulated sunlight irradiation. Obviously, all CDBSb catalysts exhibit enhanced photocatalytic degradation rates than pure BiSbO4. In comparison with other CDs modified photocatalyst, such as, CQDs/Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/TiO2 (Table S1), the CDs-BiSbO4 composites exhibits an excellent photocatalytic degradation performance [41,42]. Especially, for 2 CDBSb, its photocatalytic degradation efficiency is highest and achieves 91% in 100 min. However, for pristine BiSbO4, its degradation rate is efficiency 5%. Fig. S3 depicts the apparent first-order rate constants of different catalysts. Apparently, the apparent first-order rate constant of 2 CDBSb (Fig. S3a) is about 20 times higher than that of pure BiSbO4. Considering the CDBSb catalysts may possess wider spectral response range, the light source was converted into a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter and its photocatalytic degradation curves are presented in Fig. 4b. The optimal catalyst is also 2 CDBSb and its photocatalytic degradation rate achieves 35% in 120 min. Unfortunately, pristine BiSbO4 is unable to make RhB aqueous solution decomposed because of its wide bandgap property. The first-order rate constant of 2 CDBSb is still the highest (Fig. S3b). Taking into account of dye sensitization phenomenon, another antibiotic pollutant CIP was utilized to evaluate photocatalytic performance of the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 4c, under the illumination of simulated sunlight, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency over 2 CDBSb is about 2 times higher than that over pristine BiSbO4 and its first-order rate constant (Fig. S3c) is 2.5 times as high as that of pristine BiSbO4. Under the irradiation of a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter, 2 CDBSb still keeps the optimal performance for the degradation of CIP, as displayed in Figs. 4d and S3d. The reason why excessive CDs (>2.0 wt%) resulted in a significant decrease in photocatalytic activity is that the higher CDs content in the composite material may cause internal filtering effects by competing with BiSbO4 to absorb photons [36]. In addition, excessive CDs may hinder the surface adsorption of organic pollutants on the composite photocatalyst.

Fig. S4a is UV–vis absorption spectra of RhB under simulated sunlight and Fig. S4b is its UV–vis absorption spectra under the irradiation of a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter. Obviously, the maximum absorption peak gradually shifts to higher value, demonstrating the molecular structures of RhB have undergone a series of changes, not just a simple physical adsorption. Furthermore, LC–MS technique was utilized to analyze the photocatalytic degradation intermediates of RhB and CIP (Figs. S5 and S6) according to pervious works [43–48]. As depicted in Fig. 5 , the degradation of RhB molecules have two pathways. Pathway 1 is summarized as follows: Firstly, RhB molecules (m/z = 443) are attacked by hydroxyl radicals and loss an ethyl group to form the structure of P1 (m/z = 515). Then, further degradation makes the cleavage of 2 benzene rings and forms the structure of P3 (m/z = 243.9). Pathway 2 is described as follows: a benzene ring of RhB molecule is directly broken into the structure of P2 (m/z = 311.1) with the attack of superoxide radicals. The structures of P2 and P3 will be decomposed into intermediates (P4–P9) with lower molecular weight. Ultimately, these intermediates are gradually decomposed in to CO2 and H2O.

The degradation pathways of CIP molecules are displayed in Fig. 6 . The degradation process is described as follows: Firstly, hydroxyl radicals directly oxidize the CIP molecule and make the piperazine ring opened. Then, the dialdehyde derivative structure with m/z of 361.9 is formed. This structure is further oxidized by both hydroxyl and superoxide radicals and decomposed into two isomers of monoaldehyde moiety (m/z = 334). Next, further degradation makes the monoaldehyde moiety possibly transformed along two ways. One way is the substitution of hydroxy group, which produces P4 (m/z = 318). Another way is the loss of an additional CO, which generates the structure of P3 (m/z = 306.1). Additional degradation transforms P4 into P5 (m/z = 273) via the process of decarboxylation. P6 (m/z = 337.8) comes from oxidation of P3 by superoxide radicals and two carbon-oxygen double bonds are formed. Addition oxidation of P6 produces P7 (m/z = 255.6). Ultimately, P5 and P7 are mineralized into other intermediates.

Cycle experiments were carried out to study the stability of the optimal sample. As displayed in Fig. S7a, b, after 5 times utilization, the photocatalytic performance of 2 CDBSb has no obvious loss and its degradation rates of RhB and CIP still keep at 90% and 43%, respectively, demonstrating that the optimal sample possesses superior photocatalytic stability. Moreover, the XRD patterns of 2 CDBSb used for 5 times and the fresh one is presented in Fig. S8. In comparison with the fresh one, the crystal structure of 2 CDBSb used for 5 times has no obvious change, which also confirms its excellent stability. The remarkably enhanced photocatalytic performance discussing above may be ascribed to the effective charge separation of photoinduced electrons, which will be proved by the following analyses of optical and photoelectronic chemical tests.

Fig. 7 displays the UV–vis DRS spectra of the catalysts. The absorption edge of pristine BiSbO4 is about 350 nm. In comparison with pristine BiSbO4, all A–S samples exhibit obviously enhanced visible light absorption. Moreover, the absorption edges of A–S samples display a prominent red shift. From the inset of Fig. 7a, with the introduction of CDs, the color of pristine BiSbO4 changes from white color to black-gray color, which is consistent with the changes of their optical absorption. From Fig. 7b, the bandgap energy of pristine BiSbO4 is 3.55 eV. Besides, the positive value of the slope in the straight part of the Mott-Schottky plot in the inset of Fig. 7c represents the n-type semiconductor of BiSbO4. In combination with the measured results and valence band binding energy spectrum of pure BiSbO4 (Fig. 7c), the specific positions of VBM (valence band maximum) and CBM (conduction band minimum) are determined, as seen in Fig. 7d. The CBM potential of pure BiSbO4 is −0.35 eV, which is more negative than the potential of generating O2 − (−0.33 eV). The VBM potential of pure BiSbO4 is more positive than the potential of generating OH (2.38 eV) [41]. Fig. 8 a presents the PL spectra of pristine BiSbO4 and CDBSb samples. Not surprisingly, 2 CDBSb catalyst exhibits obvious lower peak intensity than that of pristine BiSbO4, signifying the introduction of CDs can effectively inhibit the recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes. As shown in Figs. 8b, c and S9a, b, no matter under the irradiation of simulated sunlight or a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter, the photocurrent signals of 2 CDBSb are much stronger than pristine BiSbO4 and other CDBSb composites. It is worth mentioning that pristine BiSbO4 has no any photocurrent signal under the irradiation of a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter, which is in accordance with our previous report. Meanwhile, in Figs. 8d and S9c, 2 CDBSb has a weaker EIS signal in comparison with pristine BiSbO4 and other CDBSb composites. The results of photocurrent and EIS demonstrate that the introduction of CDs can accelerate the migration of photoinduced electrons, thus making more electrons and holes generated on the surface of catalyst. More electrons and holes will cause more reactive oxygen species generated in the photocatalytic process, which can be also validated by the following DFT calculations.

XPS technique was utilized to ascertain element chemical compositions and valence states of samples. As depicted in Fig. S10, BiSbO4 are mainly composed of C, Bi, O and Sb elements. Fig. 9 display the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, Bi 4f, O 1s and Sb 3d, respectively. As presented in Fig. 9a, the peak located at 284.8 eV in pure BiSbO4 is ascribed to the XPS instrument itself possessing carbon hydrogen. Differently, for 2CDBSb, C 1s are obviously split into three different peaks. The peaks positioned at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV and 287.9 eV are ascribed to the C–C, C–O and CO, respectively, which further demonstrates some new functional groups exist in CDs/BiSbO4 composites after decorated with CDs [20]. As shown in Fig. 9b, two peaks at 158.2 and 163.5 eV belong to Bi 4f orbitals of Bi3+ for BiSbO4. Another two weaker peaks shifting to 158.8 and 164.1 eV are ascribed to Bi 4f orbitals of Bi3+ for 2 CDBSb [49,50]. In Fig. 9c, O 1s can be deconvoluted into three peaks. The binding energies positioned at 529.3 eV and 529.9 eV belong to hydroxy groups adsorbed on the surface of BiSbO4 and 2 CDBSb, respectively. Another two peaks at 530.8 eV and 531.4 eV belong to lattice oxygen for BiSbO4 and 2 CDBSb, respectively. The additional one peak in pure BiSbO4 or 2CDBSb of Fig. 9c belongs to 3d orbital of Sb5+ because O 1s and Sb 3d are overlapped with each other at the position about 530.3 eV. As depicted in Fig. 9d, for pure BiSbO4, the binding energies located at 529.4 and 538.8 eV are the characteristic peaks of 3d orbitals of Sb5+ for pure BiSbO4. The binding energies shifting to 530.0 and 539.4 eV are attributed to 3d orbitals of Sb5+ for 2 CDBSb. Similarly, in Fig. 9d, additional two peaks in pure BiSbO4 or 2CDBSb belong to the characteristic peaks of O1s. It is worth noting that in comparison with pure BiSbO4, the binding energies for 2 CDBSb all shift to higher values (about 0.6 eV), suggesting that electrons in BiSbO4 side are inclined to transfer to CDs side due to the existence of a strong electron screening effect between the interlayer of CDs and BiSbO4 [43].

For more intuitively observe the electron transfer tendency from atomic scale, DFT calculations were carried out. Fig. S12a–d are optimized structures of BiSbO4, CDs and CDs-BiSbO4 layer. Fig. 10 a, b are TDOS (total density of states) and PDOS (partial density of states) of BiSbO4 optimized primitive cell. It is clear that the CBM of BiSbO4 is constituted of Bi 6s orbital and the VBM of BiSbO4 is primarily composed of O 2s orbital theoretically, signifying the photoinduced electrons may originate from the O 2s orbital and then transfer to the Bi 6s orbital. The calculated bandgap of BiSbO4 is about 3.13 eV, which is a little smaller than the experimental value because GGA functional usually underestimates the bandgap value of semiconductors [51,52]. Fig. S11 displays the optimized cells of BiSbO4, CDs and CDs/BiSbO4 layer. Fig. 10c is the charge distribution difference at the interlayer of CDs-BiSbO4. The yellow color is on behalf of the electron accumulation region and the green color represents the electron depletion region. Apparently, C atoms gain rich electrons from Bi and O atoms in BiSbO4 side, forming an electron depletion layer at the BiSbO4 side of the interlayer. The inset in Fig. 10c is ELF (electronic location function) of CDs-BiSbO4 layer. C atoms exhibit obvious higher electron location degree than that of O atoms, signifying strong interaction exists between C atoms and O toms by the possible formation of C–O bond. From the planar averaged charge distribution plot along Z axis (Fig. 10d), we can observe more clearly that at the interface region, CDs (positive value) seize rich electrons from BiSbO4 (negative value). More interestingly, when O2 molecules are adsorbed on the surface of CDs (Fig. 10e, f), all electrons located at the CDs-BiSbO4 layer are prone to accumulate on O2 molecules, which creates a great condition for the reduction of O2 molecules to O2 −. As a result, an ultrahigh electron migration tunnel is constructed between CDs-BiSbO4 layer and O2 molecules, which is of great benefit to the transfer of photoinduced electrons from BiSbO4 to CDs and ultimately, to O2 molecules. The XPS results are in accordance with the DFT calculations.

The O2 adsorption of a photocatalyst is an essential step for the activation of molecular oxygen. Fig. 11 a and c compare the optimized models of one O2 molecule adsorbed on BiSbO4 (020) and CDs (002) surfaces. Obviously, the adsorption energy (Eads) of O2 onto BiSbO4 (−0.23 eV) was more positive than that onto CDs (−0.338 eV), demonstrating the O2 adsorbed on the CDs is more stable than that on BiSbO4 [53–55]. The results also indicate that the introduction of CDs on the surface of BiSbO4 is in favor of the activation of molecular oxygen, thus enhancing its photocatalytic degradation performance. Work function (φ) is another important parameter for studying electron transfer in semiconductor composites. It can be estimated from the energy difference between the vacuum level and the Fermi level based on the electrostatic potential of the material. As displayed in Fig. 11b and d, the work function of BiSbO4 adsorbed with O2 and CDs adsorbed with O2 are 5.64 eV and 4.32 eV, respectively. When they contact with each other, electrons will flow from BiSbO4 to CDs to enable the phases at the same Fermi level. These results are also consistent with the XPS results and of great benefit to the charge separation and O2 photoactivation.

Considering these active free radicals are important influential factors, capturing experiments of them were carried out and displayed in Fig. S12a, b. Obviously, either under simulated sunlight or a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter, both IPA, BQ and NaC2O4 can greatly inhibit the photocatalytic degradation performance of 2CDBSb, indicating O2 − and h+ play major roles in the photocatalytic process. Considering the addition of BQ may affect the absorbance of RhB solution, we conducted a comparative experiment, which is shown in Fig. S13. The result demonstrated the addition of BQ has no great influence on the absorbance of RhB. In addition, the formation of OH is derived from the holes directly oxidizing H2O molecules. For more intuitively observation O2 − and OH, the signals of them are detected by ESR technique, which are displayed in Fig. 8. Under simulated sunlight illumination, both signals of superoxide radicals and hydroxy radicals for 2 CDBSb are remarkably stronger than those of for pristine BiSbO4 (Fig. 12 a, b). Fig. 12c displays the semi-quantitative O2 − concentration generating under simulated sunlight irradiation. Obviously, 2 A-S can induce more superoxide radicals during the photocatalytic process, which is about 3 times as many as those of induced by pristine BiSbO4. Differently, under the irradiation of a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter, pristine BiSbO4 is unable to induce the generation of neither O2 − nor OH (Fig. 12d, e). However, for 2A–S, both O2 − and OH can come into generation and its O2 − concentration is displayed in Fig. 8f. Besides, considering O2 − can react with h+ to form singlet oxygen (1O2) [55–57], we also detected the ESR signal of 1O2, which is displayed in Fig. S13. From the result, no any signal of 1O2 was detected, demonstrating 1O2 cannot be generated and participate the photocatalytic degradation process. The ESR detection result is in well consistent with the result of photocatalytic capturing experiments.

Based on the trapping experiment and relative results discussing above, a possible photocatalytic mechanism is proposed (Fig. 13 ). Firstly, under simulated sunlight irradiation, wavelength less than 350 nm can make electrons in the valence band of BiSbO4 excited to its conduction band. Meanwhile, because of the up-converted property of CDs, wavelength ranging from 550 to 900 nm is partially converted into short wavelength ranging from 320 to 350 nm. The converted short wavelength can also make electrons in the valence band of BiSbO4 excited to its conduction band. The excited electrons then transfer from the conduction band of BiSbO4 to CDs. Subsequently, electrons arriving at CDs can reduce O2 molecules to form O2 −. These O2 − radicals will eventually attack pollutant molecules and ultimately make them decomposed into intermediates or CO2 and H2O. Meanwhile, holes remaining in the valence band of BiSbO4 will oxidize H2O to form OH which can also make pollutant molecules degraded into CO2 and H2O. However, under the irradiation of a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter, only the converted short wavelength ranging from 320 to 350 nm plays works, which explains the reason why the photocatalytic performance under the irradiation of simulated sunlight is obviously better than the photocatalytic performance under the irradiation of a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter.

The photocatalytic degradation rates of RhB and CIP over the optimal sample under simulated sunlight are 18 and 2 times higher than those over pristine BiSbO4, respectively. Meanwhile, under the irradiation of a Xe lamp with 550 nm cut-off filter, owing to the superior up-converted property of CDs, the optimal sample still displays excellent photocatalytic performance while pristine BiSbO4 has no any degradation effect. Moreover, the optimal composite exhibits excellent chemical stability and still keeps a great photocatalytic degradation performance after 5 times utilization. DFT calculations demonstrate photoinduced electrons originate from O 2s orbitals (VB of BiSbO4) and then excited to the Bi and Sb hybrid orbitals (CB of BiSbO4) and ultimately arrive at C 2s orbitals of CDs. The enhanced photocatalytic performance is also ascribed to the effective charge separation, making more O2 − and OH generated during the photocatalytic process. This work not only discloses the up-conversion role of CDs in a wide bandgap semiconductor such as BiSbO4, but also paves a way for fabrication of highly efficient photocatalyst with wide spectral response.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC No. 51472194) and the NSF of Hubei Province (2016CFA078).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.129460.

ce:section.ce:para:
